source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
## T2: Mechanical accelerator

A massless thread makes $N$ turns around statically fixed cylinder, as shown in the figure. Initially, the free (unwound) ends of the thread are parallel to the axis $X$. Then, a heavy point-like object $P$ is attached to one end of the thread while the other end is pulled with a constant velocity $u$ along $X$. Find the maximum velocity attained by the heavy object.

<image_1>

The thread is inextendable and flexible. Suppose that the turns of the thread are wound tightly to one another and are placed practically in the same plane, perpendicular to the cylinder axis. Neglect any friction in the system. Do not consider the force of gravity.

image_dependency: Question explicitly says 'as shown in the figure'
Problem setup depends on spatial/geometric relations: thread turns around a cylinder, free ends parallel to axis X, turns in a plane perpendicular to the axis
An image asset exists and registry_passed=true, so the referenced figure is available
multi_step: This is a mechanics/kinematics derivation asking for a maximum velocity, which typically requires combining geometric constraint relations with motion analysis
The setup includes several interacting conditions: N turns, inextensible thread, fixed cylinder, one end pulled at constant velocity u, and a heavy point mass on the other end
Finding a maximum implies more than direct lookup; it likely requires deriving velocity as a function of configuration and then identifying its peak
Answer is a closed-form expression in N and u rather than a direct visual readout
verifiability: Provided answer is a precise symbolic formula: v_{max}=u(2\pi N+1)
Multiple-choice options include a matching closed-form candidate, supporting objective checking
registry_passed=true with no listed asset issues

decision_reasons: question, image, and answer assets are all present with no registry issues
high image dependency is supported by an available figure, so core semantics are intact
multi-step and verifiability scores are high, indicating strong candidate value
the only noted risk is symbolic equivalence checking, which affects evaluation complexity but not candidate recoverability

Text and image together make the setup understandable.
